When God has fulfilled his promises to us, he expects that we acknowledge it as evidence of his faithfulness. And our earthly comforts are especially sweet when we see them coming from the promise. Whoever offered the first portion of their harvest must remember and acknowledge the humble beginnings of the nation they belonged to. My father was a Syrian about to die. Jacob is called a Syrian here. Their nation, in its early years, lived in Egypt as foreigners, serving there as slaves. They were a poor, despised, oppressed people in Egypt; and even though they became rich and powerful, they had no reason to be proud, secure, or forgetful of God. He must thankfully acknowledge God’s great kindness to Israel. The comfort we find in our own possessions should lead us to be thankful for our share in public peace and abundance; and with present blessings, we should bless the Lord for past blessings we remember, and future blessings we expect and hope for. He must offer his basket of first-fruits. Whatever good thing God gives us, it is his will that we use it as comfortably as possible, tracing its source to the Fountain of all comfort.

How can the earth yield its harvest unless God blesses it? What comfort can we find in it then? All of this represented the covenant relationship between a reconciled God and every true believer, along with the privileges and duties that belong to it. We must be watchful and show that according to the grace covenant in Christ Jesus, the Lord is our God, and we are his people, waiting in his appointed way for the fulfillment of his gracious promises.

Moses here emphasizes the commands. They are God’s laws, so you should do them; they were given for that purpose; do them, and do not question them; do them, and do not hesitate; do them, not carelessly or hypocritically, but with your whole heart and soul. We deny ourselves and break the most sacred commitment if, after choosing the Lord to be our God, we fail to make a habit of obeying his commands. We are chosen for obedience; selected that we should be holy; purified as a special people so that we might not only do good works but be zealous in them. Holiness is true honor, and the only way to everlasting honor.
